---
title: "Why two-stage and joint 4PL agree on z and CTmax even though u, low and k vary"
author: "Daniel Noble"
date: 2026-06-12
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
    theme: cosmo
    code-fold: show
    embed-resources: true
---

## The question

In the shrimp and zebrafish case studies the joint 4PL fits estimate upper and
lower asymptotes ($u$, $\ell$) and a slope ($k$) that differ across temperatures
and groups, yet $z$ and $CT_{max}$ come out *roughly the same* as the classical
two-stage pipeline. If the curves genuinely differ in $u$, $\ell$ and $k$,
shouldn't the derived quantities differ more than they do?

## Short answer

**$z$ and $CT_{max}$ are *location* quantities; $u$, $\ell$ and $k$ are *shape /
scale* quantities, and in the 4PL the two are (by construction) separable.** The
midpoint sub-model

$$
\text{mid}(T) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 (T - \bar T)
$$

is the $\log_{10}$-time at which each curve crosses the point halfway between its
own asymptotes. Under the **relative** threshold the package reads $z$ and
$CT_{max}$ *only* from this sub-model:

$$
z = -\frac{1}{\beta_1}, \qquad
CT_{max,\,t_\text{ref}} : \text{mid}(T) = \log_{10} t_\text{ref},
$$

so $u$, $\ell$ and $k$ **do not enter the calculation at all** — they can change
(even with temperature) without moving $z$ or $CT_{max}$. The classical
two-stage reads an *absolute* 50 % LT50 per temperature, and that coincides with
the relative midpoint whenever the asymptotes sit near 0 and 1, which is the case
for these datasets. So the two pipelines land on the same midpoint trajectory and
agree.

## Mechanism

Why the asymptotes and slope don't leak into $z$/$CT_{max}$:

- **Asymptotes ($u$, $\ell$) set the vertical range** of the dose-response curve.
  The *relative* midpoint is defined as halfway between $\ell$ and $u$, so it
  rides up and down with them — its **time location is unchanged** when the
  asymptotes stretch.
- **$k$ sets the steepness** of the transition. A steeper or shallower sigmoid
  still crosses its own midpoint at the same $\log_{10}$-time. $k$ governs the
  *precision* of an LT50 estimate (steeper $\Rightarrow$ tighter), not its
  location.
- Hence the midpoint $\text{mid}(T)$ — and therefore $z$ (its slope) and
  $CT_{max}$ (where it crosses $\log_{10} t_\text{ref}$) — is decoupled from
  $u$, $\ell$, $k$. This is exact under the relative threshold: the supplement
  notes $\log_{10}\text{LT}(T) = \text{mid}(T)$ is *exactly* linear, "demonstrated
  with a simulation where $u$ and $k$ carry strong $T$ effects".

The two-stage instead reads the **absolute 50 %** time per temperature,
$\log_{10}\text{LT}_{abs} = \text{mid} + \dfrac{\log(2u-1)}{k}$ (for $\ell=0$).
The extra term is the gap between "halfway between asymptotes" and "absolute
50 %". When $u \approx 1$ it is $\approx 0$ and the two thresholds coincide.

## Small demonstration

$z$ from the relative midpoint is invariant to $u$ and $k$; the absolute-50 %
$z$ only departs when the asymptotes **vary with temperature**.

```{r}
#| message: false
Tbar <- 34; b0 <- 1.5; b1 <- -0.15     # truth: z = -1/b1 = 6.667
temps <- seq(30, 38, by = 2)
mid   <- b0 + b1 * (temps - Tbar)      # log10 relative-midpoint LT(T)

z_of <- function(logLT) unname(-1 / coef(lm(logLT ~ I(temps - Tbar)))[2])

# absolute-50% LT (low = 0):  log10 LT_abs = mid + log(2u - 1)/k
abs_lt <- function(u, k) mid + log(2 * u - 1) / k

z_relative           <- z_of(mid)                                  # joint, relative
z_abs_u_constant_1   <- z_of(abs_lt(u = rep(0.999, 5), k = 8))     # u~1, constant
z_abs_u_constant_low <- z_of(abs_lt(u = rep(0.80,  5), k = 8))     # u=0.80, constant
z_abs_u_drifts       <- z_of(abs_lt(u = seq(0.95, 0.70, length = 5), k = 8))  # u falls with T

round(c(truth                = -1 / b1,
        relative_midpoint    = z_relative,
        absolute_u_near_1    = z_abs_u_constant_1,
        absolute_u_const_0.80= z_abs_u_constant_low,
        absolute_u_drifts    = z_abs_u_drifts), 3)
```

Reading the output:

- `relative_midpoint` $= 6.667$ **exactly**, regardless of $u$ and $k$ — this is
  the joint 4PL's $z$ under the relative threshold.
- `absolute_u_near_1` $= 6.667$: with asymptotes at 0/1 the absolute LT50 *is* the
  midpoint, so the two-stage matches.
- `absolute_u_const_0.80` $= 6.667$ too: a **constant** sub-unit asymptote shifts
  every LT50 by the *same* amount, so the slope (hence $z$) is unchanged — only
  the intercept (hence $CT_{max}$) shifts a little.
- `absolute_u_drifts` $\neq 6.667$: once $u$ **changes with temperature**, the
  shift changes with $T$, tilting the absolute-LT50 line and biasing $z$.

So the agreement needs only two benign conditions, both met by shrimp and most
zebrafish stages: **(i)** asymptotes near 0/1 (so absolute $\approx$ relative for
$CT_{max}$), and **(ii)** asymptotes/slope roughly **stable across the assay
temperatures** (so $z$ is untilted). Neither requires $u$, $\ell$, $k$ to be
identical across temperatures or groups — only that their *temperature drift* is
mild.

## Where they do diverge (and the data show it)

- **Young zebrafish embryos** have an upper asymptote well below 1
  (~0.76; background mortality unrelated to heat). There the absolute and
  relative thresholds part company, and the threshold-comparison figure in the
  supplement shows the two $CT_{max}$ definitions separating for that stage while
  the other stages stay on top of each other.
- **The simulations** deliberately drive $u$ (and $k$) to drift with temperature
  (scenarios 3, 6, 7). That is exactly the `absolute_u_drifts` case above: the
  two-stage $z$ and $CT_{max}$ pick up systematic bias, while the joint 4PL on the
  relative threshold stays unbiased "because the classical model cannot represent
  changing asymptotes directly".

## What actually differs in the benign case

Equal point estimates do **not** mean the methods are interchangeable. In the
benign regime the joint 4PL and two-stage agree on the *value* of $z$ and
$CT_{max}$, but differ in:

- **Uncertainty.** The two-stage treats each Stage-1 LT50 as a fixed, independent
  point in the Stage-2 regression, so its intervals are mis-calibrated; the joint
  posterior propagates the full covariance (this is what the bias-coverage
  simulations quantify — joint coverage stays ~0.95, two-stage drops well below).
- **Robustness.** When conditions are *not* benign (sparse data that don't bracket
  the midpoint, asymptotes drifting with $T$), the two-stage point estimates also
  break, while the joint fit absorbs the asymptote/slope variation and keeps the
  midpoint clean.

This is the paper's "equivalence as enhancement" point: the agreement on shrimp
and zebrafish is the *expected* result and a feature, not a coincidence — the
joint 4PL reproduces the classical answer where the classical answer is
trustworthy, and adds correctly-propagated uncertainty plus protection for the
cases where it is not.

## Related

`[[project_z_ctmax_derivation]]`, `[[project_tcrit_definition]]`,
`[[feedback_equivalence_framing]]`,
`[[reference_lit_tdt_dynamic_core]]` (Rezende landscape; $z$ from the midpoint
slope).
